citation.sourceDocument: Delaware Division of Revenue tax-rate schedule (30 Del. C. §1102, brackets for tax years 2014 and later); 30 Del. C. §1108 standard deduction
citation.sourceNote: Delaware's seven-tier rate schedule is identical for every filing status (30 Del. C. §1102, unchanged for tax years 2014 and later): 0% on the first $2,000 of taxable income, then 2.2% over $2,000, 3.9% over $5,000, 4.8% over $10,000, 5.2% over $20,000, 5.55% over $25,000, and a top rate of 6.6% over $60,000. Crucially, Delaware does NOT double the brackets for joint filers — both spouses' income runs through the same schedule, a built-in marriage penalty, so a joint return owes nearly as much as a single one at equal income (the only relief is the larger standard deduction). The standard deduction is the statutory $3,250 single / married-filing-separately / head-of-household and $6,500 married filing jointly (30 Del. C. §1108, unindexed and unchanged since 2000); federal head-of-household filers use Delaware's single figure. Delaware grants no personal exemption — it was replaced by a nonrefundable $110-per-exemption personal credit (and an extra $110 for filers age 60+), which is omitted at launch fidelity like every state's credits, so the figure errs slightly high (by at most $110-220), the conservative side for setting money aside. The additional standard deduction for filers who are blind or age 65+, and Delaware's itemized-deduction option, are likewise omitted. HB 13 (153rd General Assembly) would add 6.75% and 6.95% brackets above $125,000/$250,000 and restructure the lower tiers effective tax year 2026, but it was not enacted (the 2025 session adjourned without passing it), so the statutory schedule above remains in force; if HB 13 or a successor is signed, the brackets become the reviewer's data-only step. Cross-checked against the Tax Foundation 2026 state-rate table; verified against revenue.delaware.gov and delcode.delaware.gov on 2026-06-06.
